original_title: Development of a High-Throughput Size-Exclusion Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry Workflow for Real-Time Monitoring of Bioconjugation of Cysteine-Based Antibody–Drug Conjugates
title: Real-time ADC bioconjugation monitoring with SEC-MS
blurb: A size-exclusion chromatography-mass spectrometry workflow that watches cysteine-based ADC conjugation as it happens, giving you drug-to-antibody ratios without waiting for end-point analytics. High-throughput means you can screen linker and payload combinations faster. Whether that's actually where your bottleneck is—conjugation rather than formulation, manufacturing, or animal testing—is another question.
blurb_long: A size-exclusion chromatography-mass spectrometry workflow that watches cysteine-based ADC conjugation as it happens, giving you drug-to-antibody ratios without waiting for end-point analytics. High-throughput monitoring means you can screen linker and payload combinations faster than endpoint assays and catch conjugation failures before they're baked in. For shops already iterating on DAR and linker chemistry, that feedback loop is useful, since you get direct readouts instead of waiting for QC analytics. But conjugation kinetics are one small piece of ADC development, and most timelines are constrained elsewhere: protein production, formulation stability, or animal testing. Still, if that's where you're stuck, the convenience is real.

original_title: Synthesis and in vivo evaluation of a bifunctional, glutamic acid derived chelator for the 203/212Pb theranostic pair
title: A lead chelator tuned by charge engineering
blurb: Glutamic acid-derived bifunctional chelator for Pb-203/212 theranostics, with charge optimization to improve pharmacokinetics. Charge engineering is a sensible design principle, though lead chelators are hardly in short supply.
blurb_long: Glutamic acid-derived bifunctional chelator for Pb-203/212 theranostics, with charge optimization to improve pharmacokinetics. Charge engineering is a sensible design principle, though lead chelators are hardly in short supply. The logic is straightforward: if a series of chelators can differ mainly in their net charge, you can optimize biodistribution for a given target without major structural redesign. But that only works if the different charges actually produce meaningful differences in vivo, and building up enough variants to optimize across is real work. Whether this approach solves a bottleneck in lead-212/-203 theranostics or mainly adds another option to a space that usually isn't constrained by chelator choice is what the data need to show.

original_title: Overall Survival with [177Lu]Lu-PSMA-617 Versus [177Lu]Lu-PSMA I&T: A Propensity Score-Matched Real-World Analysis
title: Lu-177 PSMA-617 and PSMA I&T, matched head to head on survival
blurb: A propensity-matched real-world comparison of the two Lu-177 PSMA ligands on overall survival, which is the question the registrational trials never ran directly against each other. Matching is not randomization and the usual real-world confounders apply, but it is the closest read we have on whether the ligand choice actually moves the outcome.
blurb_long: A propensity-matched real-world comparison of the two Lu-177 PSMA ligands — PSMA-617 and PSMA I&T — on overall survival, the head-to-head the registrational trials never actually ran. Matching is not randomization and the usual real-world confounders apply, but it is the closest read anyone has on whether the ligand choice moves the outcome rather than just the biodistribution. The interesting part is that these two agents get used almost interchangeably in practice, on supply and reimbursement grounds, as if the choice were cosmetic. If a survival gap holds up here, that assumption is wrong and the whatever-is-in-the-hot-cell-today approach needs rethinking. Treat the magnitude with suspicion — matched cohorts flatter whichever arm had the healthier patients — but the question it forces is the right one.
